text:
COMBINED RESTAGING EXAMINATION
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST AND MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Yuen is a 64-year-old woman with known Stage IV non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma histology, right upper lobe primary, with EGFR L858R mutation. She has a history of prior osimertinib therapy (first-line, approximately 14 months) with documented acquired resistance manifest as systemic progression in the chest and three new intracranial metastases. Brain metastases were treated with stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) to all three sites (left frontal 12 mm, right cerebellar 8 mm, left parietal 6 mm) approximately 9 weeks prior. She subsequently initiated carboplatin plus pemetrexed chemotherapy and has now completed three cycles. This examination is performed for restaging to assess treatment response.

TECHNIQUE:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Multidetector CT was performed in the portal venous phase following intravenous administration of iohexol 350 (90 mL) via right antecubital vein access. Axial images were acquired from lung apices through the pubic symphysis at 1.25 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reconstructions in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Lung window, mediastinal window, and soft tissue window reconstructions reviewed. Comparison to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 9 weeks earlier (at time of progression from osimertinib), and original staging PET-CT.

MRI Brain: Standard brain MRI protocol performed on a 3 Tesla system with the following sequences: axial T1, T2, FLAIR, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), and gadolinium-enhanced T1 sequences (axial, coronal, sagittal). Gadolinium contrast (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg) administered intravenously. Comparison to prior MRI brain performed approximately 9 weeks prior (immediately following progression diagnosis, pre-SRS).

---

CT CHEST FINDINGS:

Lungs and Airways:
The trachea and central bronchi are patent and midline without endobronchial lesion or extrinsic compression. The right upper lobe continues to harbor a spiculated, irregular soft tissue opacity centered in the posterior segment. This lesion measures approximately 2.1 x 1.7 cm in maximal cross-sectional dimensions on the current study, compared to 2.7 x 2.2 cm on the prior examination approximately 9 weeks ago. The lesion demonstrates mild interval decrease in size, with persistent spiculation and a small area of pleural tethering along the posterolateral chest wall; this tethering is unchanged and may reflect desmoplastic/fibrotic changes from prior therapy rather than active invasion. No new right upper lobe satellite nodules. No endobronchial component identified.

The remainder of the right lung is without new consolidative opacity, mass, or nodule. The left lung is clear bilaterally. No new pulmonary nodules are detected. No ground-glass opacification or interstitial change suspicious for drug-related pneumonitis.

Mediastinum and Hila:
Mediastinal contours are normal. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. Pericardium is intact with no pericardial effusion. No anterior or posterior mediastinal mass.

Right paratracheal station (4R): A right paratracheal lymph node previously measuring 1.1 cm in short axis is now decreased to approximately 0.7 cm in short axis, consistent with favorable interval change. No other pathologically enlarged right paratracheal nodes identified.

Right hilar region: Previously enlarged right hilar lymph nodes remain within normal limits, measuring up to 0.5 cm in short axis, unchanged from the prior CT.

Subcarinal station (station 7): No pathologically enlarged subcarinal lymph nodes. The largest subcarinal node measures 0.6 cm in short axis (stable).

Left mediastinal stations (4L, 5, 6): No pathologically enlarged nodes identified. Lymph nodes, where visualized, are within normal limits.

Right supraclavicular region: No enlarged lymph nodes identified in the right supraclavicular fossa on this examination. This region was not well-evaluated on the current chest CT acquisition given field of view; however, no prominent node is visualized.

Pleural Spaces:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pleural thickening. No pneumothorax.

Chest Wall and Bones:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions of the thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, clavicles, or scapulae. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine at multiple levels, age-appropriate. No pathologic fracture. The posterior right chest wall in the region of prior pleural tethering demonstrates no frank rib erosion or cortical destruction.

---

CT ABDOMEN FINDINGS:

Liver and Biliary:
The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypodense or hyperdense masses on pre-contrast or post-contrast sequences. Intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. Gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones or wall thickening.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas:
The pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and enhancement pattern. No pancreatic mass, ductal dilation, or peripancreatic fluid collection.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Both kidneys are normal in size and symmetrically enhance. No renal masses, hydronephrosis, or perinephric fluid. Simple bilateral renal cortical cysts (largest 1.2 cm, right kidney), unchanged, consistent with Bosniak I cysts and of no clinical significance.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and appearance. No adrenal nodule or mass. No new adrenal lesion. Specifically, no adrenal metastasis identified.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
Bowel loops are not distended. No bowel wall thickening or obstruction. No free intraperitoneal fluid or pneumoperitoneum. The appendix is not visualized but there are no secondary inflammatory changes.

Vasculature:
Aorta and its major branches are normal in caliber. No aneurysmal dilation. Celiac, superior and inferior mesenteric arteries are patent. No para-aortic or aortocaval adenopathy. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Peritoneum:
No peritoneal implants identified. No ascites.

---

CT PELVIS FINDINGS:

Bladder is partially distended and without wall thickening. Uterus and ovaries are not separately visualized given their atrophic post-menopausal appearance but no adnexal mass is identified. No pelvic free fluid. No iliac, obturator, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Pelvic osseous structures show no suspicious lesion.

---

MRI BRAIN FINDINGS:

General:
There is no midline shift. Ventricles are normal in size and symmetric. The basilar cisterns are open. Gray-white matter differentiation is preserved throughout. No diffusion restriction to suggest acute infarction. No abnormal susceptibility artifact to suggest intracranial hemorrhage on SWI beyond the expected post-treatment changes described below.

Known Treated Metastases:

(1) Left frontal lobe lesion (previously 12 mm prior to SRS):
The left frontal enhancing lesion has substantially decreased in size. On gadolinium-enhanced T1 sequences, residual enhancement measures approximately 7 mm in maximum dimension, representing an interval decrease from 12 mm at the pre-SRS baseline. The surrounding T2/FLAIR signal abnormality (previously reflecting vasogenic edema) has markedly diminished; only a thin rim of FLAIR signal change is seen at the periphery, in keeping with post-treatment reactive change. No new surrounding mass effect. There is a small focus of susceptibility signal at the center of this lesion on SWI, consistent with post-radiosurgical hemosiderin deposition -- an expected finding. The overall picture is consistent with a favorable local treatment response following stereotactic radiosurgery, though it is not possible to fully exclude a small viable component given persistent (though reduced) enhancement.

(2) Right cerebellar hemisphere lesion (previously 8 mm prior to SRS):
The right cerebellar lesion demonstrates residual faint peripheral enhancement measuring approximately 6 mm in greatest dimension, compared to 8 mm at the pre-SRS baseline. There is no significant surrounding T2/FLAIR edema. No mass effect on the fourth ventricle. The lesion appears stable to minimally decreased. SWI demonstrates subtle susceptibility signal at the lesion center consistent with post-treatment hemosiderin. Findings are consistent with post-radiosurgical change with stable/slightly decreased lesion size.

(3) Left parietal lobe lesion (previously 6 mm prior to SRS):
The previously identified left parietal enhancing lesion is now reduced in size to approximately 3 mm of faint punctate enhancement, a significant decrease from 6 mm. There is no surrounding edema and no mass effect. This finding is consistent with a favorable response to stereotactic radiosurgery.

Leptomeningeal Disease:
No leptomeningeal enhancement identified on gadolinium-enhanced sequences, including careful review of sulci, cisterns, cranial nerve sheaths, and ependymal surfaces. No evidence of leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

New Intracranial Lesions:
No new enhancing lesions are identified elsewhere in the brain or posterior fossa. No new T2/FLAIR signal abnormality suspicious for new metastatic deposits.

Background Brain Parenchyma:
Small scattered T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the bilateral periventricular and subcortical white matter are present and unchanged from prior, consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease. No new areas of signal abnormality. No cortical restricted diffusion.

Calvarium, Skull Base, Orbits:
No osseous destructive lesion of the calvarium or skull base. Orbits are unremarkable. Paranasal sinuses demonstrate mild mucosal thickening in the bilateral ethmoid sinuses, likely representing chronic inflammatory changes of no acute clinical significance.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. RIGHT UPPER LOBE MASS -- DECREASED IN SIZE: The right upper lobe primary lung mass has decreased from 2.7 cm to approximately 2.1 cm in longest dimension compared to the prior examination obtained at the time of progression from osimertinib. This represents a modest but meaningful interval reduction in the primary tumor in the setting of three cycles of carboplatin and pemetrexed chemotherapy. The lesion remains notably smaller than the original presentation at diagnosis (5.8 cm).

2. RIGHT PARATRACHEAL LYMPHADENOPATHY -- DECREASED: The right paratracheal (4R) lymph node has decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm in short axis, consistent with treatment response in the mediastinal nodal compartment.

3. NO NEW THORACIC, ABDOMINAL, OR PELVIC LESIONS: No new lung nodules, hepatic lesions, adrenal metastases, or osseous lesions are identified. The abdomen and pelvis remain clear of metastatic disease. No evidence of new peritoneal involvement.

4. INTRACRANIAL METASTASES -- FAVORABLE RESPONSE FOLLOWING SRS: All three previously identified brain metastases demonstrate interval size reduction following stereotactic radiosurgery. The left frontal lesion has decreased from 12 mm to 7 mm with significant reduction in surrounding vasogenic edema. The right cerebellar lesion is stable to mildly decreased (8 mm to 6 mm). The left parietal lesion has markedly decreased from 6 mm to 3 mm. No new intracranial metastases are identified. No leptomeningeal disease.

5. NO INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE OR RADIATION NECROSIS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE INTERVENTION: Post-treatment susceptibility changes are identified at the treated lesion sites, consistent with expected hemosiderin deposition following radiosurgery. No frank radiation necrosis or symptomatic treatment-related imaging changes identified at this time.

Overall, the findings are consistent with a partial response to systemic chemotherapy in the chest, with concomitant favorable local response to stereotactic radiosurgery in the brain. No evidence of new systemic or intracranial metastatic disease.
